# Chapter 13

[pjapfe9f] The plan wasn’t drawn on paper, because paper felt too honest for what they were doing. Cage paced the shop with a stolen tablet in his hand, flicking through satellite angles of a neighborhood that looked like it had been designed by a committee of dentists. The roofs were flat. The yards were green in a way the desert never was. Even at this distance, Flynn could see the geometry of money.

[3img15j2] Cage spoke like he was calling a race. Entry line here, exit line here, apex at the place where the cameras got bored and the guards got used to boredom. He had the home layout from a hacked realtor archive and the security system schematic from a contractor forum that didn’t know it was a confession booth. “People build fortresses,” Cage said, “and then they pay strangers to talk about them online.”

[pcqcqum2] Birdie leaned over the screen, fingers tapping, eyes bright with that terrible joy he got when the world turned into a puzzle. “There’s a service alley,” Birdie said. “Trash pickup. Water filtration intake. We could come in under the shrubs.” He said shrubs like they were part of nature and not a costume. Flynn watched him and tried to remember the kid who’d once gotten excited about carburetors.

[8hyd7ui7] Wrench stayed near the door, half in shadow, cigarette unlit between his fingers. Every few minutes he put it in his mouth and forgot to light it. “This isn’t a car,” he said at last. “This is a man.” Cage didn’t even look up. “He’s a password,” Cage said. “He’s a key fob with meat around it.”

[dhbou5mu] Flynn felt Diesel’s absence like a missing tooth. The compound sounded wrong without her moving through it, without her voice turning fantasies into numbers. Cage had said she’d stay behind to “keep the place alive,” as if she were a generator. Flynn knew the real reason: Diesel still believed in lines you didn’t cross, even in a world that kept erasing consequences.

[tiug7bnd] They picked the night like thieves pick weather. No moon. Wind enough to smear footprints. A forecast of dust after midnight that would turn lenses into blindness. Cage didn’t call it luck. Cage called it planning. Flynn watched him lay out zip ties, a roll of duct tape, a little injector pen of sedative Birdie had “borrowed” from a clinic kiosk.

[dbkojn5o] “We don’t use that unless we have to,” Flynn said, and hated how small his voice sounded. Cage glanced at him, eyes shiny and calm. “We use whatever works,” Cage said. “We’re not making art.” Birdie nodded too fast. Wrench rubbed his face hard enough to leave red marks.

[s290i7pm] The safe house was an abandoned survey station out past the last cell tower, a concrete rectangle with a cracked roof and a door that had been kicked in years ago and never forgiven. They’d found it on an old federal map, the kind that still showed places the government had once pretended to care about. Cage liked it because it was lonely. Flynn hated it because it was lonely.

[hopkh4oo] The roles were simple, because complicated things gave you room to think. Cage would go hands-on. Birdie would handle restraints and screens. Flynn would be support—whatever that meant when the thing you were supporting was violence. Wrench would drive the van and watch the street, eyes on any neighbors brave enough to still look out windows. “We’re in and out,” Cage said, like he was talking about stealing batteries.

[mcfujbye] Wrench asked, “And then what?” It was a quiet question, almost polite. Cage smiled without warmth. “Then he transfers one-fifty,” Cage said. “Then he opens the garage.” He said garage the way a man says heaven. “Then we leave him somewhere that he can be found. He gets a story for his therapist. We get our fuel money.”

[sb3bsje1] Flynn tried to picture Victor Khoury as anything other than a headline: billionaire, tech savior, pod evangelist, a man who’d funded revival clinics in poor neighborhoods to wash his hands in gratitude. In the files Cage had pulled, Khoury looked ordinary in the way rich men did when they wanted to seem safe. Early forties. Wife. Two kids. Photos in matching clothes at some beach that had been cleaned for them.

[0p9kkc7l] “Don’t start,” Cage said, reading Flynn’s face like a mirror. “He’s not a person to you. He’s a system. The system made us. The system kept us hungry. The system can pay.” Flynn swallowed. He wanted to argue that systems were made of people, but his tongue felt thick. Birdie bounced on his heels, as if this were the night before prom.

[j9v457hm] At sundown, the compound turned quiet in the way it always did before a run, the brief truce between heat and dark. The Shelby sat under its tarp like a sleeping predator. Flynn checked the van twice: plates swapped, radio dead, windows coated with a film that would make cameras see only their own glare. He kept waiting for Diesel to step out and say stop.

[8z3hhd3h] Diesel didn’t come to the yard. She stayed in the shop with the lights off, just a cigarette ember moving like a firefly. Flynn paused by the door, hearing the hum of the battery bank. “You okay?” he asked. Diesel’s answer came after a long moment. “No,” she said. “But I’m not going with you.” Then softer: “If you come back, you’re going to be different.”

[i8hvgac8] They left at full dark, the van’s tires whispering over packed dirt. The highway was a ribbon of old asphalt with new cracks stitched into it. Cage sat in the passenger seat, feet on the dash, humming something tuneless. Birdie in the back kept checking the sedative pen as if it might vanish. Wrench drove with both hands welded to the wheel, jaw tight.

[p99v25m9] Phoenix at night was a sea of light pretending to be a city. The rich districts were brighter, cleaner, guarded by drones that looked like sleek insects. They slid into Khoury’s neighborhood through service roads and dark gaps between manicured walls, the van painted the same beige as a thousand delivery vehicles. Flynn watched his own reflection in the side glass and didn’t recognize the eyes.

[4a32lv4w] They parked half a block away behind a line of artificial palms that had never known thirst. The air smelled like chlorine and cut grass. Somewhere a fountain ran for no reason. Cage pulled a thin mask over his face, the kind you wore when you didn’t want your implant to read emotions. Flynn hesitated, then did the same, feeling the fabric seal against his cheeks like a lie.

[ot4qyqmn] The side gate lock was smart, but smart locks trusted that people would behave. Birdie plugged in a little coil of wire, fingers steady, and whispered to the gate in a language of pulses. It clicked open like it had always been theirs. “See?” Birdie mouthed, grinning. Flynn’s stomach dropped as if the ground had shifted.

[2xsc4b30] The backyard was a postcard: pool glowing blue, patio lights dimmed to romantic, a grill covered like a shrine. The house itself was glass and stone, too clean to have ever held a real argument. Cage moved fast, hugging shadows, one hand on a compact stun baton. Flynn followed, feeling the absurdity of tiptoeing across perfect tile like a burglar in an old movie.

[jx6vckt0] A camera tracked them once, then drifted away, fooled by Birdie’s looped feed. The quiet was heavy. Flynn heard his heartbeat through his own skull. Cage reached the sliding door and paused, listening. Inside: low voices, a laugh, the clink of dishes. Normality. Flynn thought, almost wildly, that they should turn back because the people inside were still living in the world where you could be safe.

[47f1z79n] Cage didn’t wait for Flynn to decide. He slid the door open an inch, then two, then stepped through like smoke. The living room was all pale fabric and art that looked like it had been chosen because it wouldn’t offend. Victor Khoury stood at the kitchen island with a glass of wine. His wife was rinsing plates, hair tied up, barefoot.

[4gu2spls] The moment stretched because nobody expects the desert to walk into their house. Khoury’s eyes widened, but he didn’t scream. He reached for the counter where his implant remote sat beside a fruit bowl. Cage crossed the room in two steps and hit him with the baton. The sound was wet and final. Khoury folded, gasping, wine spilling like blood over white stone.

[9r0z56y1] Khoury’s wife screamed then, a sharp animal sound that made Flynn’s skin crawl. Birdie was on her immediately, arms around her from behind, trying to pin her without hurting her, whispering, “It’s okay, it’s okay,” like he believed words could make it true. Flynn moved to help without thinking, hands shaking, feeling the strange intimacy of touching a stranger’s terror.

[gnh5nhwe] Somewhere deeper in the house, a child cried out, confused and angry. A door thumped. Footsteps. Flynn’s head snapped toward the hallway and saw a small figure in a pajama shirt with cartoon rockets, face crumpled with sleep. Another smaller shape behind him. The boy’s eyes fixed on Cage and Khoury on the floor and the scream started to build.

[rpdaqo5f] “No,” Flynn said, and it came out like a plea. Cage’s voice cut through the room, calm as a traffic update. “Birdie, tape. Flynn, hallway.” Flynn moved like he’d been programmed. He stepped into the hallway and held out his hands, palms open, trying to be a wall that didn’t look like a threat. The boy recoiled anyway.

[58i9761v] “Hey,” Flynn said, forcing softness into his voice. “Hey, it’s okay. Go back to bed.” The words were insane. The boy stared at the baton in Cage’s hand and at his father’s body twitching on the tile. The little one behind him began to wail. Khoury’s wife fought harder, nails tearing at Birdie’s arm. Flynn felt the night snap into something irreparable.

[uomj1wqx] Khoury tried to stand. He was stronger than Flynn had imagined, stronger than the footage. He grabbed Cage’s wrist, teeth bared, and for a second Flynn saw not a billionaire but a man who loved his own life. Cage’s eyes sharpened with annoyance. He hit Khoury again—hard, across the cheekbone. The crack echoed off glass. Khoury went down, blood blooming at his mouth.

[1bqarp5k] The kids’ crying turned into a siren. Cage swore under his breath. “Move,” he said. Birdie fumbled tape around the wife’s wrists, crying himself now, whispering apologies. Flynn crouched, caught the boy’s shoulders, and held him back from running into the room. The boy thrashed, small fists hitting Flynn’s chest. Flynn wanted to let him go. Flynn didn’t.

[kib5s2x3] “Please,” Khoury rasped from the floor, spitting blood. “Please, I have children—take anything—take the cars, take the house—just don’t—” His voice broke on the last word, the one he couldn’t say because saying it might make it real. Flynn’s throat tightened so hard it hurt. Cage knelt beside Khoury like a doctor and spoke into his ear.

[ui44ky7d] “We’re taking the cars,” Cage said. “And you’re transferring one hundred fifty thousand dollars. Then you’re free.” He said it like terms of service. Khoury’s eyes found Flynn’s, begging, as if Flynn were the only person in the room who still remembered what mercy was. Flynn looked away, cowardice disguised as strategy.

[01ezrv8y] Wrench’s voice crackled in Flynn’s earpiece. “Neighbors,” Wrench hissed. “Porch light. Movement.” Cage stood, decision already made. “Out,” Cage said. Birdie hauled Khoury up under the arms. Khoury’s knees buckled. Cage yanked him upright by the collar and shoved him toward the back door. Flynn released the boy, who stumbled into the wall, sobbing.

[r34vkm8g] The wife surged forward, reaching for her husband, and Birdie jerked her back. Flynn caught her by the elbow, meaning to help, meaning to keep her from running into whatever came next. Her eyes met his for half a second—pure hatred, pure memory. Flynn felt the shape of his own face carved into her mind. He thought, too late, about masks and names.

[qhzz6qxf] They dragged Khoury across the patio, past the glowing pool, past the grill, past a children’s bicycle tipped on its side like a crime scene marker. The night air hit Khoury and he began to shake uncontrollably. Wrench had the van doors open, engine running. His eyes went wide when he saw Khoury’s bleeding mouth and the way Cage held him like luggage.

[50lfkex9] “Jesus,” Wrench breathed, and it wasn’t a prayer so much as an accusation. Cage shoved Khoury into the back and climbed in after him. Birdie followed, panting, tape stuck to his fingers. Flynn jumped into the passenger seat beside Wrench. In the side mirror he saw the house’s glass wall lit up, silhouettes moving frantic behind it. No sirens yet. Just the sound of a family learning a new kind of fear.

[d6kl3ge3] They drove fast and careful, the way you drove when you were trying to outrun consequences. Through the rich lights, onto darker roads, toward the desert where cameras died and the city’s attention thinned. Khoury made choking noises in the back. Cage told him to breathe. Birdie said, “We’re not going to hurt you,” over and over, like repetition could rewrite what had already happened.

[1opsvain] The safe house smelled of old piss and sun-baked concrete. They carried Khoury inside, half walking, half dragging, and sat him on a broken chair. Birdie wrapped zip ties around his ankles and wrists. Khoury flinched at every touch. Flynn stood near the doorway, listening to the wind scrape grit across the roof, thinking of the kids’ faces like bruises behind his eyes.

[blfh6cka] Cage set a small folding table in front of Khoury and put a cracked laptop on it. A hotspot puck blinked weakly, reaching for the last fringe of signal. “You have a finance app,” Cage said. “Everyone does. Open it.” Khoury shook his head, not defiant, just broken. “No,” he whispered. “No, please.”

[mls5ayq0] Cage’s patience evaporated like spilled fuel. He grabbed Khoury by the hair, tilted his head back, and pressed the stun baton’s tip against Khoury’s throat without firing it. The gesture was intimate and obscene. “Open it,” Cage said softly. “Or I call your wife and tell her the next time she sees you will be in pieces.” Birdie’s face went white. Flynn felt his hands curl into fists he didn’t use.

[8yiaj331] Khoury sobbed then, the sound of a man who’d spent his life believing money was armor. Tears ran through the blood on his lip. He nodded, frantic, and Birdie cut the zip tie on one wrist just enough for Khoury to work the screen. Khoury’s fingers shook so badly he kept missing the icons. Cage watched him like a hawk watches a mouse.

[ghmiy459] The transfer took three minutes and felt like a year. One hundred fifty thousand dollars slid from one account to another with a cheerful confirmation tone, as if it were a pizza order. Cage leaned in and dictated the routing numbers like he’d done this his whole life. When the screen flashed SUCCESS, Cage clapped Khoury on the shoulder—almost friendly—and said, “See? Nobody has to die.” Flynn looked at Khoury’s eyes and knew something in him had already died anyway.
